_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows the curriculum in Runestone Foundations of Python Programming, Think Python, and OpenStax Introduction to Python Programming. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the sources guide topic selection only.
title: Dictionaries
summary: Store related values under names that describe what they mean.
refresher: A dictionary connects each unique key to a value. Read or update with square brackets, test membership with `in`, and use `get` when a missing key should return a fallback instead of raising KeyError.

blocks.body: A list answers questions by position: item zero, item one, item two. A dictionary answers by meaning. A student record can keep a name under `"name"` and a credit count under `"credits"`, so the code explains what each value represents.

blocks.caption: Keys appear before colons and values appear after them. Square brackets use a key to find its value.

blocks.body: Assigning an existing key replaces its value. Assigning a new key adds another pair. Keys must be unique, while several keys may point to equal values.

blocks.caption: Bracket lookup is useful when a key is required. `get` is better when absence is normal and you have a sensible fallback.

blocks.title: Membership checks keys
blocks.body: `"Ada" in student` looks through the keys, not the values. Use `"name" in student` to ask whether that field exists. Use `"Ada" in student.values()` only when you truly need to search values.

blocks.why: Updating `pen` does not create a second key. Adding `book` makes two keys total, and the current value stored under `pen` is 3.

blocks.body: Looping over a dictionary gives its keys. Use `.items()` when a loop needs each key and value together. Unpacking gives both clear names on every pass.

blocks.prompt: Which loop prints both each course name and its grade?
blocks.why: `items()` produces key-value pairs that can be unpacked into two variables. Iterating over the dictionary alone produces only keys.

blocks.title: Dictionary checklist
items: Use brackets when a missing key is an error.
Use `get` for an optional value with a fallback.
Use `.items()` when a loop needs keys and values.
Remember that assigning the same key updates rather than duplicates it.
